description: FERC approves ISO tariff, market rules, and major design changes. Jurisdictional authority over wholesale markets.
source: FERC Order No. 2000 (RTOs/ISOs); Order No. 888 (open access). https://www.ferc.gov/power-sales-and-markets/rtos-and-isos

description: FERC approves transmission rates and Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT). Order 1000 mandated regional planning."
source: FERC Order No. 888 (OATT); Order No. 1000 (transmission planning & cost allocation). https://www.ferc.gov/electric-transmission/order-no-1000-transmission-planning-and-cost-allocation

description: FERC oversight of NERC under EPAct 2005 — approves NERC's reliability standards, sanctions enforcement actions, and may direct standards development.
source: EPAct 2005 / FPA section 215; FERC Order No. 672. https://www.ferc.gov/reliability-explainer

description: FERC regulates wholesale generator conduct: market power mitigation, market-based rate (MBR) authority (including for bilateral wholesale sales), must-offer rules, anti-manipulation, asset divestiture conditions.
FERC regulates LSE wholesale market behavior — participation in capacity, energy, and ancillary services markets; cost allocation under FERC tariffs.

FERC regulates financial market participation across cleared markets and bilateral OTC instruments: anti-manipulation (Order 670), capital/credit requirements for virtual and FTR trading, affiliate transaction rules.
FERC approves DAM design including offer caps, mitigation, and clearing rules.
FERC approves RTM design, scarcity pricing constructs, and operating reserve demand curves where applicable.
source: FERC Order No. 719 (operating-reserve shortage / scarcity pricing). https://www.ferc.gov/sites/default/files/2020-06/OrderNo.719.pdf

description: FERC approves AS product definitions and procurement rules.
FERC approves capacity market design including accreditation, mitigation, and demand curves. Site of major contestation (MOPR, ELCC reforms).
FERC approves FTR auction design and SFT methodology.
FERC approves ARR allocation formula in ISO tariff.
FERC sets OATT transmission service rates, transmission revenue requirements, and approves RMR contracts. Order 1000 mandated regional planning and cost allocation.
FERC sets interconnection process rules. Order 2023 mandated cluster studies and other reforms.
State PUCs regulate LSE certification, retail conduct, and procurement obligations (IRP filings, RPS compliance reporting).
State PUCs set retail rate structures, consumer protections, and distribution-level rules.
State PUCs approve retail tariffs and distribution rates. Primary venue for ratemaking proceedings.
State PUCs handle distribution-level interconnection (FERC handles transmission-level).

State PUCs hold siting authority over in-state transmission lines and review certificates of public convenience and necessity (CPCNs). The state/federal seam in transmission expansion — contested under FERC's Order 1000 backstop siting authority.
NERC reliability standards constrain ISO operations (e.g., BAL standards on balancing authority performance).
NERC reliability standards constrain TO planning and operations (e.g., TPL planning standards).
NERC GO/GOP standards apply to generator owners and operators (e.g., PRC protection coordination, MOD modeling requirements).
State legislatures set the statutory framework PUCs operate within. RPS, clean energy mandates, ratemaking principles.
State policies (RPS, carbon pricing pass-through, retail competition rules) directly bind LSEs as the obligated entity.
ISO operates DAM and sets implementation rules within the FERC-approved tariff.
ISO operates RTM and dispatches in real-time.
ISO operates AS markets, sets product specifications and procurement quantities.
ISO runs capacity auction, sets accreditation rules, demand curves, locational constraints.
ISO runs FTR auction, performs SFT, defines feasibility region.
ISO administers ARR allocation per tariff formula.
ISO runs interconnection studies, sets queue rules, allocates network upgrade costs.
Cleared capacity carries a must-offer obligation: committed resources must offer into DAM, constraining the supply curve.
Generators submit supply offers (energy curves, start/min-run constraints). Defines the supply side of SCUC.
LSEs submit demand bids (price-sensitive or self-scheduled) to procure next-day energy on behalf of load.
Virtual bidders submit financial increments and decrements to arbitrage the DAM-RTM seam; FTR traders may submit DAM bids tied to congestion positions.
Generators follow ISO dispatch instructions and may update offers in real-time within tariff limits.
LSEs self-schedule load and operate DR programs and wholesale-passthrough retail products on behalf of customers; settle imbalance against RTM LMPs.
Generators (and storage, hybrid resources) offer reserve and regulation capability into co-optimized energy/AS clearing.
Generators (and increasingly storage, hybrid resources) offer capacity supply obligations into the auction.
Financial traders bid on path-based congestion rights, taking positions against expected DAM congestion patterns.
LSEs self-convert ARRs to FTRs or bid in the FTR auction to hedge congestion exposure on their load.
LSEs nominate ARRs against their load-and-transmission-cost obligations and choose to take cash or self-convert to FTRs.
Generators submit interconnection applications, fund studies, negotiate LGIAs and network upgrade cost allocation.
Consumer advocates and individual large customers intervene in PUC proceedings — rate cases, RPS dockets, large-load policy.
Large-load and data-center interests intervene at FERC on interconnection, cost allocation, and colocation rules.
Large loads negotiate corporate PPAs and on-site or colocation supply deals with generators, reducing the net power they draw from the grid.
Generators file complaints, intervene in proceedings, request tariff changes, file market-based rate updates.
LSEs intervene in tariff filings, file complaints over cost allocation, participate in capacity market proceedings.
Financial traders intervene in FTR and virtual-bidding proceedings, file complaints over uplift and revenue adequacy.
LSEs file rate cases, IRPs, RPS plans, DR program filings, and tariff updates at PUCs.
TO conducts interconnection studies (jointly with ISO), constructs network upgrades, executes LGIAs.
TOs file OATT rate cases, transmission revenue requirement updates, formula rate filings, and intervene in regional planning and cost allocation proceedings.

Generators originate, negotiate, and execute bilateral supply contracts: PPAs (physical and financial), tolling agreements, bilateral RA, asset-backed marketing. The principal channel through which merchant economics translate into revenue.
LSEs procure bilateral supply: long-dated PPAs (often RPS-compliant), tolling, bilateral RA for capacity, and basis hedges. The primary hedge for retail load obligations beyond ISO product offerings.
Financial traders enter CFDs, basis swaps, heat-rate options, and other OTC instruments to take or offset risk positions without holding physical assets.
Generators pay network upgrade construction costs directly to TOs per interconnection cost allocation. Project-finance flow with no ongoing settlement.

Generators pay the ISO their net cleared-market obligations: storage/hybrid charging energy (DAM and RTM), capacity penalties for non-performance, interconnection study deposits, and other ISO-administered charges. The ISO is the legal counterparty for centrally-cleared transactions.
LSE-side bilateral PPA payments — physical PPAs, tolling, bilateral RA. The principal long-dated hedge for retail load.

LSEs pay the ISO their net cleared-market obligations: DAM cleared energy at LMP_load, RTM imbalance buys, AS uplift, capacity charges (load-share of system peak — 4CP/5CP/NSPL by ISO), FTR auction buys, and OATT/transmission-service uplift. The single largest outflow on an LSE's balance sheet.

Financial traders pay the ISO for buy-side FTR auction positions, net-negative virtual settlements (when increment/decrement positions clear unfavorably), and any revenue-inadequacy uplift assessments.
End customers pay retail bills to LSEs under PUC-approved retail tariff structures. The retail tariff determines the amount; the cash flow is direct.
Payments into corporate PPAs and colocation supply agreements.

ISO pays generators their net cleared-market revenues: DAM/RTM energy clearing at LMP_gen, AS awards, capacity revenues. The ISO collects from buyers across all cleared markets and pays sellers from the aggregate pool; congestion rent — the residual between LMP_load and LMP_gen at congested constraints — funds FTR settlement.
ISO pays LSEs ARR cash distributions (per the ARR formula applied to FTR auction revenue), FTR settlements on LSE-held FTRs, RTM credits for DR and curtailment, and occasional uplift credits.
ISO pays traders FTR congestion settlements (when path positions align with realized DAM congestion) and net-positive virtual settlement revenues.
ISO remits OATT transmission revenue requirement collections to TOs as cost-of-service compensation.
Bilateral PPA settlements to generators — physical PPAs, tolling, bilateral RA. Settled financially against DAM LMPs; willingness-to-pay is shaped by capacity revenues (the missing-money debate).
ISO supplies network model, constraint set, loss factors, contingency list to DAM clearing optimization (SCUC).
ISO supplies network model and real-time state estimation to RTM clearing (SCED).
ISO supplies network model to FTR auction clearing (SFT). Modeling choices here drive revenue adequacy.
ISO supplies capacity accreditation values (ELCC, etc.) and locational deliverability constraints.
ISO publishes prices (LMPs, capacity clearing, AS prices), dispatch instructions, settlement statements, and market notices to generators.
ISO publishes prices, settlement statements, capacity obligations, and market notices to LSEs.
ISO publishes prices, FTR auction results, and settlement statements to financial traders.
ISO sends outage approvals, dispatch coordination, and post-contingency operating instructions to TOs.
ISO reports market outcomes, performance metrics, and proposed tariff changes.
NERC submits proposed reliability standards, compliance reports, and event analyses to FERC for approval and oversight.
TO provides network topology, equipment ratings, outage schedules to ISO for use in market clearing models.
DAM clearing produces LMPs and cleared positions. These outputs flow back to the ISO, which uses them as the basis for settlement and as inputs to RTM, FTR settlement, and downstream processes.
RTM clearing produces 5-minute LMPs and dispatch quantities used by the ISO for imbalance settlement.
AS clearing prices and awarded quantities flow back to the ISO for settlement and dispatch.
Capacity auction results (clearing prices, cleared positions) flow back to the ISO for billing and obligation tracking.
FTR auction results (cleared positions, clearing prices) flow to the ISO, which collects auction revenue and uses cleared positions to settle against DAM congestion.
ARR allocation outputs (ARR awards by LSE and path) flow to the ISO, which uses them to disburse ARR cash and to determine self-conversion eligibility in the FTR auction.
Interconnection outputs (queue position, study results, signed LGIAs, network upgrade plans) flow to the ISO and update the resource model used by DAM/RTM/capacity.

DAM unit commitment — which generators are committed for each hour, including minimum runs and ramp schedules — is the binding input to RTM dispatch. RTM (SCED) optimizes 5-min dispatch within the DAM-committed envelope; it cannot generally re-commit units in real time.
Energy and AS are co-optimized; AS clearing prices reflect the opportunity cost of providing reserves vs. energy.
Real-time energy and AS are co-optimized; AS deployment occurs in real-time.
Historical load data from RTM settlements feeds the ARR allocation formula.
Transmission cost allocation under OATT feeds the ARR allocation formula.

OATT tariff schedule determines the transmission charges the ISO collects from transmission users (generators, LSEs, traders, and transmission-level large loads) and remits to TOs. The mechanical price signal behind the OATT/TRR money flow.
FTR auction revenue is the funding source for ARR allocation distributions. Both flows pass through the ISO, but the structural dependency is FTR-auction-revenue → ARR-pool.
DAM congestion clearing produces the congestion rents (collected by the ISO from load) that fund FTR settlements. Revenue inadequacy emerges when these rents don't fund cleared FTRs in full.
DAM LMPs are the standard settlement reference for most bilateral hedges, PPAs, and CFDs. The DAM-bilateral link is what makes the OTC layer financially coherent.
Capacity market revenues shape bilateral PPA willingness-to-pay and the structure of capacity-inclusive vs energy-only contracts — the missing-money question lives in this edge.
Retail tariff structure determines what customers owe: passes through wholesale energy, capacity, transmission, ARR credits, and policy adders.

State PUC actions — IRP outcomes, OOM (out-of-market) payments, state subsidies, retail-side resource preferences — create signals the capacity market design must accommodate. The MOPR debate lives in this edge.
State RPS targets, clean-resource mandates, and decarbonization goals drive capacity accreditation reforms (ELCC methodology fights, clean-resource carve-outs).

A transmission-level load settles wholesale charges directly with the ISO: energy at the LMP, ancillary-service uplift, capacity, and transmission. Each layer appears as a separate charge rather than one blended rate.
source: FERC Order No. 888 (open access); Order No. 2000 (RTOs/ISOs). https://www.ferc.gov/power-sales-and-markets/rtos-and-isos

description: A load served through an LSE pays one bundled rate, often under a negotiated large-load tariff. The wholesale layers are blended together and the LSE holds the market instruments.
Credits paid back to a transmission-level load: payments for real-time curtailment and settlements on any congestion rights it holds.
Prices, settlement statements, and capacity obligations published to a directly-connected load.
For an LSE-served load, the retail tariff sets the bundled rate — a blended pass-through of wholesale energy, capacity, and transmission charges.
A flexible load can cut consumption when the real-time price is high. Loads that value uptime highly, such as data centers, rarely curtail on price alone.
source: FERC Order No. 719 (demand response in energy markets). https://www.ferc.gov/sites/default/files/2020-06/OrderNo.719.pdf

description: A load that can curtail quickly could sell that ability as an operating reserve. Most markets do not yet allow demand-side reserves, so this is rarely compensated today.
source: FERC Order No. 719; Order No. 2222 (DER aggregation). https://www.ferc.gov/media/ferc-order-no-2222-fact-sheet

description: A transmission-level load can hold congestion rights (ARRs and FTRs) itself instead of relying on an LSE.
Large loads increasingly go through interconnection study and pay for network upgrades — an emerging process whose rules are still being set.
Large loads negotiate special retail tariffs and intervene in state proceedings over large-load cost allocation.
FERC regulates a transmission-level load's wholesale participation and is writing large-load interconnection and cost-allocation rules.
State PUCs set special retail tariffs for large loads served through an LSE.
